title: Brown stew chicken
subtitle: Jamaican caramelised chicken braise
excerpt: Brown stew chicken is the Jamaican home kitchen braise: chicken pieces browned deeply in caramelised sugar until the meat is dark and sticky, then slow-cooked with onion, scotch bonnet, thyme, and allspice until the sauce is thick and glossy. The browning is the step that makes it.
sourceNotes:
• Levi Roots, *Reggae Reggae Cookbook* (2008). Principal reference for the sugar-browning technique and the seasoning blend.
• Riaz Phillips, *Belly Full: Caribbean Food in the UK* (2021). Cross-reference for British-Caribbean home kitchen method and the low-and-slow finish.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool fully, transfer to a sealed container, and freeze for up to 3 months. Defrost overnight in the fridge and reheat gently on the hob, adding a splash of water if the sauce has thickened.
recipe.batchNotes: The recipe scales directly. Double the ingredients and use a larger pot; browning will take two batches to avoid steaming.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The stew improves by a day in the fridge. Cool fully, refrigerate, and reheat on the hob with a splash of water.

glossaryTerms.definition: In Jamaican cooking, browning refers to caramelising sugar in hot oil before adding the chicken. The caramelised sugar coats the meat in a dark, slightly bitter glaze that gives brown stew chicken its characteristic colour and depth. Not to be confused with Maillard browning, though both happen here.

content.text: The colour of brown stew chicken comes from

: sugar dropped into hot oil and left until it turns the colour of dark treacle before the chicken goes in. The sugar is not a sweetener — it caramelises past sweet into something bitter and complex, coating the chicken in a dark glaze that nothing else replicates. The technique is foundational in Jamaican home cooking, and it is what distinguishes this dish from a straightforward chicken braise.

The rest of the dish is patient: onion, scotch bonnet, thyme, and allspice cooked down into a thick sauce that clings to the chicken. Bone-in thighs give the best result because they hold up to an hour of gentle simmering without drying. Serve with rice and peas, fried plantain, or plain steamed rice.
A wide, heavy cast-iron casserole or Dutch oven with a lid gives even heat for both the browning stage and the long simmer. Thinner pans can scorch the sugar before it evenly coats the chicken.

Heat {{vegetable-oil}} of vegetable oil in a wide, heavy casserole over a medium-high heat. Add {{granulated-sugar}} of sugar to the hot oil. Watch: it will melt and then darken. Leave it without stirring until the sugar turns a very deep amber — almost black at the edges. This takes 2 to 3 minutes. The smoke is normal; the colour should be darker than you think is right.

Working quickly, add the thighs skin-side down. The caramel will spit and seize around the chicken — keep calm. Turn to coat all sides in the darkened sugar. Brown for 3 to 4 minutes per side until the skin is deeply coloured. Work in batches if needed; do not crowd the pan. Set the browned pieces aside.

Reduce the heat to medium. Add {{onion}} sliced onion and {{scotch-bonnet}} chopped scotch bonnet to the pot. Cook for 8 to 10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the onion softens and picks up some of the caramel left in the pot. Add {{garlic}} of garlic and cook for 1 minute. Add {{thyme}} thyme sprigs, {{allspice}} of allspice, {{black-pepper}} of black pepper, and {{soy-sauce-dark}} of dark soy sauce. Stir to combine.

Return the chicken to the pot. Pour in {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock. The liquid should come halfway up the chicken pieces; top up with water if needed. Bring to a gentle simmer, cover, and cook on the lowest heat for 35 to 40 minutes until the chicken is completely tender and pulling from the bone. Remove the lid for the last 10 minutes to let the sauce reduce and thicken. Taste for salt, rest for 5 minutes, then serve.

items.symptom: Sugar burns before the chicken goes in
items.cause: The heat was too high or the sugar was left unattended past the right moment.
items.fix: If the sugar turns truly black and smells acrid rather than caramel-bitter, discard and start again — burnt sugar is bitter beyond use. Keep the heat at medium-high and watch constantly once the sugar starts to melt.

items.symptom: Chicken skin is pale rather than dark
items.cause: The caramel was not dark enough before the chicken went in, or the chicken was moved too frequently.
items.fix: The sugar must reach a deep amber before the chicken is added. Once the chicken is in, leave it — moving it prevents the glaze from setting.

items.symptom: Sauce is thin at the end
items.cause: Too much stock was added, or the lid was kept on throughout.
items.fix: Remove the lid and increase the heat slightly for the final 10 minutes. The sauce should reduce to a glossy, spoon-coating consistency.

items.cause: Boneless pieces were used, or the simmer ran too hot.
items.fix: Bone-in thighs are essential for this braise. If the heat is too high, the cooking time shortens and the meat can tighten before the connective tissue has softened.

content.text: Brown stew chicken is one of the everyday dishes of Jamaican home cooking, made weekly across the island and in Caribbean households in the UK. In Britain it arrived with the Windrush generation and has remained a constant in kitchens from Brixton to Handsworth. The caramelised sugar technique is unfamiliar to anyone who did not grow up eating this food, and it is what makes the dish immediately recognisable. It is a braise by method but distinctly Caribbean by character.
